recipes.title: Bandeja Paisa
recipes.description: Colombia's mountainous platter from Antioquia: beans, rice, chicharrón, chorizo, ground beef, fried egg, plantain, avocado, and arepa.
steps: Cook soaked beans in fresh water with a piece of plantain, garlic, and onion for 90 minutes until tender.
Score pork belly skin, simmer in salted water 30 minutes, dry, then deep-fry until crackling — this is the chicharrón.
Brown ground beef with onion, tomato, garlic, and cumin until cooked through. Season well.
Grill or pan-fry chorizos until cooked and golden.
Pan-fry plantain slices in oil until caramelized on both sides.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in butter or oil.
Plate generously: beans, rice, chicharrón, ground beef, chorizo, fried plantain, fried egg, avocado, and an arepa for each person.

recipes.title: Arepas
recipes.description: Colombian griddled corn cakes made from precooked white cornmeal — eaten with butter, cheese, eggs, or as a base for toppings.
steps: In a bowl, combine masarepa with salt.
Slowly add warm water, mixing with a spoon, then knead by hand for 2 minutes into a smooth, soft dough.
Knead in melted butter and grated cheese if making cheese arepas.
Cover with a damp cloth and rest 5 minutes — the dough should be moist but not sticky.
Divide into 8 portions, roll into balls, and flatten into 1 cm thick discs.
Cook on a dry, hot griddle or non-stick skillet over medium heat 5 minutes per side until lightly charred.
Serve hot, split, with butter or stuffed with cheese, scrambled eggs, or shredded chicken.

recipes.title: Sancocho
recipes.description: Hearty Colombian soup of meats, plantains, yuca, and corn, simmered with cilantro and served with avocado and rice.
steps: In a large pot, cover beef ribs with 3 liters of water and bring to a boil; skim foam.
Add onion, garlic, scallions, cumin, and salt. Simmer 60 minutes.
Add chicken pieces and corn cobs; simmer 20 more minutes.
Add yuca and green plantain chunks; simmer 15 minutes.
Add potatoes and continue cooking until tender, about 15 more minutes.
Stir in chopped cilantro and adjust seasoning.
Ladle into bowls with a piece of each meat and vegetable. Serve with white rice, avocado, and ají on the side.

recipes.title: Ajiaco
recipes.description: Bogotá's signature creamy potato soup with three varieties of potato, chicken, corn, and the unique herb guascas.
steps: Place chicken in a pot with 2.5 liters water, scallions, garlic, and salt. Simmer 25 minutes, then remove and shred chicken.
Strain the broth and return to the pot.
Add papas criollas and russet potatoes, simmer 25 minutes — they will dissolve and thicken the broth.
Add papas sabaneras and corn cobs, cook 20 minutes more until tender.
Stir in guascas during the last 10 minutes of cooking.
Return shredded chicken and stir in cilantro.
Serve in bowls topped with a swirl of cream, capers, and avocado slices on the side.

recipes.title: Lechona
recipes.description: Tolima specialty: whole roasted pig stuffed with rice, peas, and seasoned pork, slow-cooked until skin is shatteringly crisp.
steps: Soak split peas in cold water for 4 hours, then drain.
Cook rice in 1 liter water until just al dente (about 12 minutes).
Sauté onion, scallions, and garlic until soft. Stir in cumin, pepper, and salt.
Combine cooked rice, drained peas, cubed pork belly, and the aromatics in a bowl. Mix well.
Lay the butterflied pork shoulder skin-side down. Spread the rice-pea filling over the meat, then roll tightly and secure with twine.
Place skin-side up in a roasting pan. Score the skin in a diamond pattern and rub with extra salt.
Roast at 160°C (320°F) for 4 hours, then increase to 220°C (425°F) for 30 minutes to crisp the skin. Rest 15 minutes before slicing.

recipes.title: Patacones
recipes.description: Colombian twice-fried green plantains, smashed flat and crisped — a beloved side or snack with hogao or guacamole.
steps: Peel green plantains by scoring the skin lengthwise and prying it off.
Slice plantains into 3 cm thick rounds.
Heat oil to 160°C (320°F) and fry plantain rounds for 4 minutes — they should be tender but pale.
Remove and drain briefly on paper towels.
Smash each round flat to about 5 mm thick using the bottom of a glass or a tostonera.
Soak smashed pieces briefly in salted water with garlic for extra flavor and crispness; pat dry.
Increase oil temperature to 190°C (375°F) and fry patacones again for 2 minutes until deeply golden and crisp. Drain, salt, and serve.

recipes.title: Empanadas
recipes.description: Colombian empanadas with a crisp yellow corn-flour shell filled with seasoned beef and potato, deep-fried golden.
steps: Boil potato until very tender, drain, and lightly mash. Set aside.
Brown ground beef with onion, scallions, garlic, cumin, and tomato until cooked. Season with salt and combine with mashed potato.
Mix masarepa, salt, and annatto, then knead in warm water until smooth and slightly tacky. Rest 5 minutes.
Take a small ball of dough, place between two plastic sheets, and press flat into a thin disc.
Spoon filling into the center, fold the dough into a half-moon, and seal edges firmly by pressing.
Trim excess dough with a small bowl rim for a clean edge.
Heat oil to 180°C and fry empanadas in batches 4–5 minutes until deeply golden. Drain and serve with ají picante.

recipes.title: Buñuelos
recipes.description: Colombian Christmas-season cheese fritters made from queso costeño and cornstarch — round, golden, and irresistible.

ingredients.name: queso costeño or queso fresco, finely grated

steps: In a large bowl, combine grated cheese, cornstarch, sugar, baking powder, and salt.
Add the egg and milk, mixing until a smooth, soft dough forms; if dry, add a splash more milk.
Knead briefly until the dough is uniform and slightly tacky.
Roll the dough into smooth balls about 4 cm in diameter — keep them perfectly round for even frying.
Heat oil to a low temperature (about 150°C) — this is critical so the buñuelos cook through without darkening too quickly.
Add buñuelos and fry slowly, turning, for 5–7 minutes until they puff and rotate on their own.
Increase heat slightly at the end to deepen the color, then drain on paper towels and serve warm.

recipes.title: Tamales Tolimenses
recipes.description: Banana-leaf-wrapped corn dough parcels stuffed with chicken, pork, peas, carrots, and rice — a hearty Tolima Sunday tradition.
steps: Marinate chicken and pork ribs with onion, garlic, scallions, cumin, annatto, and salt for at least 4 hours.
Soak rice and peas overnight, then drain. Mix masa with 500 ml of warm broth into a thick batter.
Pass banana leaves over a flame briefly to make them pliable.
Lay two leaves cross-shaped, spoon a thick layer of masa in the center, top with rice, peas, a piece of each meat, carrot, and potato.
Fold leaves over to form a tight rectangle and tie firmly with twine.
Stand parcels upright in a large pot, cover with water, and weigh down with a plate.
Simmer covered for 3 hours, topping up water as needed. Unwrap and serve hot with hot chocolate.

recipes.title: Mondongo
recipes.description: Hearty Colombian tripe soup slow-cooked with chorizo, vegetables, and herbs — comforting and deeply savory.
steps: Soak the cleaned tripe in cold water with lime juice and salt for 30 minutes, then rinse very well.
Place tripe and pork ribs in a large pot with 3 liters water. Bring to a boil and skim foam.
Add onion, garlic, scallions, half the cilantro, cumin, and salt. Simmer 90 minutes until tripe is tender.
Add chorizo, tomato, carrot, and potato; cook 30 more minutes until potatoes are soft.
Adjust salt and finish with the remaining cilantro.
Skim any excess fat from the surface.
Serve hot with white rice, avocado, ají, and a squeeze of lime.

recipes.title: Hogao
recipes.description: Colombia's foundational tomato and scallion sauce — a slow-simmered sofrito spooned over rice, beans, plantains, and meats.
steps: Heat oil in a wide skillet over medium heat.
Add white onion and scallions; cook gently 8 minutes until very soft and translucent.
Stir in garlic and cook another 1 minute until fragrant.
Add tomatoes, cumin, annatto, and salt. Stir well to combine.
Reduce heat to low and simmer uncovered for 25–30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until tomatoes break down into a thick, glossy sauce.
Stir in cilantro and adjust salt.
Serve warm spooned over arepas, rice, beans, fried plantains, or grilled meats.

recipes.title: Chicharrón
recipes.description: Colombian crispy pork belly strips with crackling skin — essential to bandeja paisa and a beloved snack on its own.

ingredients.name: pork belly with skin, cut into strips

steps: Score the skin of each pork belly strip in a crosshatch pattern, cutting through the fat but not the meat.
Rub strips with salt, garlic, cumin, and lime juice. Marinate at least 1 hour.
Place strips skin-side up in a heavy skillet and add water to come halfway up the meat.
Bring to a boil, reduce to medium, and simmer 30–40 minutes until water has fully evaporated.
Once dry, the rendered fat will start to fry the meat. Continue cooking, turning occasionally, for about 15 minutes.
Increase heat slightly to finish crisping the skin until it puffs and crackles audibly.
Drain on paper towels, season with extra salt, and serve hot with arepas and ají.
